video_title: Arthur Hayes: The AI Crash That Could Send Bitcoin to $1M
gist: Arthur Hayes argues an AI capex bubble (bigger than the railroads, financed on fraudulent 5-6yr GPU depreciation schedules) will implode around 2027-2028, triggering a credit event larger than 2008 and forcing money-printing that, because the Fed 'can't print Moore's law,' will flood into crypto and send Bitcoin toward $1M. Near-term he is risk-off: he sold Hype/Near/Zcash, sits in T-bills, holds Bitcoin, and warns AI is sucking all liquidity out of crypto.

themes.theme: AI Bubble Implosion & The Crypto Endgame

insights.claim: Arthur Hayes argues the AI capex buildout is financed on fraudulent accounting because GPUs are amortized over 5-6 years while chips actually improve and depreciate every ~2 years, so a forced mark-to-reality on H100/Blackwell assets will trigger a credit event larger than 2008 in the 2027-2028 window.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Watch GPU depreciation/amortization schedules in hyperscaler financing as the lead indicator of the AI credit unwind; position for a 2027-2028 dislocation.
insights.quote: You're taking a chip that depreciates every 2 years... but you're giving it a life of five or six-year life, that doesn't make any sense to amortize a loan based on that.

insights.claim: Hayes predicts that when the AI bubble implodes, central bank money printing to save the banks cannot rescue AI assets because 'the Fed can't print Moore's law,' so the liquidity will flow directly into crypto and take Bitcoin to $1M.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Frames the next major Bitcoin leg up as a post-AI-crash liquidity flight; being liquid (cash/T-bills) into the crash to buy the bottom is the trade.
insights.quote: The Fed can't print Moore's law. Can't make a depreciation schedule change no matter how much money you print. And I think that's the best argument for why crypto is going to do so well when AI completely implodes.

insights.claim: Hayes models the AI unwind on the railroad financial crises (1873, 1893) and subprime, expecting a first 'little test' possibly summer 2026, mark-to-reality starting 2027, and a fall-2028 'big print' yield-curve-control moment as the perfect storm.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Gives a datable roadmap: treat fall 2028 as the target window for maximum money-printing and the crypto bottom-buying opportunity.
insights.quote: The perfect storm for a big print yield curve control back the truck up situation would be sometime in the fall of 2028.

insights.claim: Hayes contends the optimal play is to ride the AI bubble into cash, let all assets correlate to one on the downdraft, and buy the bottom — explicitly rejecting the idea of being pre-positioned in Bitcoin/Ether before the crash since everything sells off together.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Counsels against buy-and-hold through the crash; the edge is liquidity and timing the post-crash QE inflection (S&P bottomed March 2009, months after TARP/QE).

insights.claim: Hayes frames the AI short as the defining asymmetric trade of the epoch, comparable to John Paulson's and Michael Burry's subprime bets, where correct timing means 'you'll never work again.'
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Signals where a top macro trader is concentrating conviction; the alpha is in being liquid and timing the implosion, not in any single long.
insights.quote: This is the John Paulson subprime trade. This is the big short Michael Burry trade of this particular epoch.

insights.claim: Hayes identifies the circular financing of AI (Nvidia/Google invest in a lab, the lab buys the chips, Google underwrites the credit of the data center supplying its compute) as the structural fragility that will force mark-to-reality.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Circular revenue/vendor-financing deals are the tell; watch for these unwinding as the crack that exposes AI revenue as fiction.

insights.claim: Hayes argues China will commoditize the AI inference market the way it has every other market, since it builds a gigawatt of electricity at one-sixth the US cost and DeepSeek/Alibaba/Tencent/Baidu open-source models are 'good enough' at ~1% of the price.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Implies US frontier-lab pricing power and margins collapse; bearish for OpenAI/Anthropic/US AI valuations once consumers go price-sensitive.
insights.quote: China builds a gigawatt of electricity at 1/6 the cost of the US.

insights.claim: Hayes applies a 'good enough at a fraction of cost destroys brand value' framework (BYD vs Ford, Toyota Corolla) to AI, arguing end consumers and businesses won't pay 100x for a US model when a near-free Chinese open-source model gives the same answer.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Sell-side thesis for US AI: brand premium evaporates when commodity substitutes appear; mass-market AI demand accrues to Chinese suppliers.
insights.quote: The end consumer doesn't care about brands when the competing product is good enough and is a fraction of the cost... your brand value goes to zero.

insights.claim: Hayes cites a pricing-model fragility where the $50/month all-you-can-use subscription doesn't reflect true cost, and a shift to pay-per-token/API pricing will cause usage to dry up and expose real AI revenue economics.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Watch for AI providers moving off flat subscriptions toward metered pricing as the catalyst that breaks demand/revenue narratives.

themes.theme: Anti-AI Politics as the Second Catalyst

insights.claim: Hayes predicts the 2028 US presidential election will be a referendum on AI driven by ordinary Americans' anger over job loss, dirty water, and data-center pollution while they own none of the gains — and the mere risk of a winning anti-AI candidate should make investors sell ahead.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Adds political tail-risk to AI equities; the election itself becomes a sell catalyst regardless of outcome.
insights.quote: I think the 2028 presidential election will be a referendum on AI. And it doesn't really matter who wins or loses.

insights.claim: Hayes draws a parallel to the robber-baron era: even if AI founders stay rich post-regulation (like Rockefeller after Standard Oil's breakup), ordinary secondary-market shareholders get 'battered and bruised' as capital and labor renegotiate the new economy.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Historical template warning retail AI-stock holders that political/regulatory backlash hits public shareholders hardest.

insights.claim: Ryan Sean Adams notes regulators historically attack a sector when it is weak post-bubble (crypto: Warren's anti-crypto army late 2022, Gensler 2023), implying AI regulation will arrive after the AI bubble pops as a one-two punch, not at the peak.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams
insights.so_what: Timing model: expect the heaviest AI regulatory crackdown after the crash, compounding the downside.

themes.theme: Crypto Positioning: BTC, ETH and the Liquidity Drought

insights.claim: Hayes has sold Hype, Near and Zcash to take profit and de-risk the AI-narrative trade, and is now mostly in T-bills earning ~3.5% plus a large Bitcoin position, prioritizing capital preservation over appreciation.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: A bellwether macro trader has rotated from alt longs to cash/BTC, signaling a summer 2026 topping period for risk assets.
insights.quote: I'm more concerned about capital preservation than appreciation.

insights.claim: Hayes exited Zcash specifically over the risk of a complete capital wipeout from a technical issue, and will only re-evaluate re-entry after formal verification is added and the new 'Shielded pool' launches.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Flags an outstanding unfixed technical/cryptographic risk in Zcash; the formal-verification fix and new shielded pool launch are the re-entry triggers to watch.

insights.claim: Hayes says if forced to choose one with a dollar of fiat, he'd pick Ether over Bitcoin purely on the chart, citing ETH trading ~30% below its 200-week moving average and still well below its 2022 ~$5,000 ATH while every other major (BTC, SOL) has reclaimed prior highs.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: ETH offers the cleanest mean-reversion/catch-up setup among mega-caps; pent-up demand could fuel a sharp rally if it starts moving.
insights.quote: If you give me a dollar of fiat capital and say we choose one, I'd choose Ether over Bitcoin purely just on a chart perspective.

insights.claim: Hayes explains crypto's weak cycle (BTC merely doubling from its prior ATH to ~$125k, ETH down ~50% YTD) as a direct result of globally printed liquidity being funneled entirely into AI as the 'fastest horse,' leaving crypto starved of the marginal fiat dollar.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Crypto cannot meaningfully re-rate (e.g., BTC to $500k) while AI absorbs all marginal capital; crypto's revival requires AI to stop sucking the oxygen out of the room.
insights.quote: We printed a lot of money globally, and what happened? All that capital was funneled into AI.

insights.claim: Hayes frames every asset allocation as opportunity cost against the fastest fiat-debasement hedge, noting that with AI second/third-derivative plays (e.g., SanDisk/SK Hynix up 20-50x) available, an investor has little reason to buy Bitcoin for a mere ~10x.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Explains relative-value drag on crypto; until AI's outsized returns fade, BTC remains a 'shittier version of a tech stock' for the marginal allocator.

themes.theme: Energy / Oil as the Bear Trigger

insights.claim: Hayes calls the post-Iran-truce oil selloff a fake-out and predicts WTI reaches uncomfortable levels (potentially ~$120 in 6-12 months) as governments, commercial players and previously-short nations all rush to restock 100+ days of hydrocarbon inventories.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Buy the oil-price-bottom: a structural restocking bid supports a move back toward $120 WTI even if the strait reopens.
insights.quote: I believe that this is a fake out and we're going to be at uncomfortably uncomfortable levels of hydrocarbon prices.

insights.claim: Hayes recommends the oil-restocking thesis is best expressed via ExxonMobil, oil-services funds, natural-gas companies, and Latin American energy plays within the US orbit, bought now at depressed prices.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Concrete long ideas to play a higher-probability-than-priced oil move; entry at current lows offers asymmetry.
insights.quote: I think this is a great buying opportunity for ExxonMobil, you know, oil services funds, natural gas companies. Latin America energy plays that are within sort of the US orbit.

insights.claim: Hayes warns the Iran truce is likely a temporary ruse to stick-save markets and float mega-IPOs (SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI), with renewed Iran/Israel strikes plausible after the November US elections, so the trade window may close in 60 days.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Geopolitical re-escalation risk is a near-term oil/inflation catalyst; the 60-day truce details (nuclear material, transit fees) are the surface area to monitor.

insights.claim: Hayes presents the bear case that sustained ~$110-120 oil triggers inflation and recession (US inflation already filtering through from the earlier oil spike with a ~3-month lag), making high oil prices the simplest reason to be bearish risk assets.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: High oil is the recession trigger to hedge; inflation prints in coming months reflect the prior spike, not the current dip.

insights.claim: Hayes argues oil price is largely irrelevant to AI capex up to a point because hyperscaler CEOs have signaled willingness to risk bankruptcy rather than lose the AGI race, meaning even $150 oil may not dent AI enthusiasm.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Caps the oil-as-AI-killer thesis; the AI bid persists through high energy costs, which is itself the steelman against Hayes's own AI-bear view.
insights.quote: Every single major US tech CEO has basically said, I'm okay with my company going bankrupt if I don't risk that I lose the AGI war.

themes.theme: Perpetual Swaps: Structure and Market Share

insights.claim: Hayes attributes the invention of the perpetual swap to himself and the BitMEX team in 2016 via the self-correcting funding-rate mechanism (using trailing premium/discount to set a forward rate), a fully self-contained design that made perps work in a high-leverage crypto environment.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Context on the dominant crypto derivative's design; the funding-rate mechanism is why perps, not CME-style futures, capture leverage demand.

insights.claim: Hayes argues socialized loss (your initial margin is your max loss; winners get ADL'd rather than paid from the exchange balance sheet) is the non-negotiable feature that lets exchanges offer 100x leverage on volatile bearer assets, and TradFi's clinging to guaranteed-settlement clearinghouses will make their perp launches fail.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Structural moat for crypto-native perp venues; traditional exchanges cannot match leverage without abandoning guaranteed settlement, so their perp products will underwhelm.
insights.quote: Unless you put a socialized loss mechanism into your clearing situation and your margining, then you, from a risk perspective, cannot offer this product.

insights.claim: Hayes predicts Hyperliquid will eventually flip Binance because decentralized exchanges outsource security to the L1, avoiding the escalating cost Binance bears to secure its own environment, while clients only see the fee.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Long-term bull case for Hyperliquid over centralized perp incumbents on a structural cost-of-security advantage.
insights.quote: The fundamental reason why decentralized exchanges are better is because the client pays for the security of the L1, but then pays again for the trading fee.

insights.claim: Hayes contends perps are fundamentally a retail product (24/7, 100x leverage, no futures-curve complexity), so institutions like Citadel will be forced onto Hyperliquid or the dominant perp DEX to access the retail flow they profit from.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Liquidity-follows-retail thesis predicts institutional migration to perp DEXs as a structural trend.

insights.claim: Hayes expects both an offshore winner (Hyperliquid, Binance) and an onshore US winner, but says offshore perps will be highly commoditized with thin margins as ~5-10 licenses get handed out and everyone overspends on marketing.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Onshore US perp distribution/marketing access is the differentiated prize; offshore becomes a low-margin commodity fight.

insights.claim: Hayes, the inventor of the perpetual swap, trades spot only and uses no leverage, advising that anyone not living the trade 'day in day out with alarms on your phone' should avoid leverage because crypto is already volatile enough to generate good returns.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: A credibility signal on leverage risk; he prefers earning yield by staking/lending to the perp market only when funding rates are high.
insights.quote: This thing is already so volatile, you don't need leverage to make good returns.

themes.theme: AI Equity Market Health & IPO Pipeline

insights.claim: Hayes characterizes the SpaceX IPO as a 'low-float high-FDV shitcoin' that has run in its first two trading days but faces unlocks starting September, with Anthropic and OpenAI also coming to market — a setup he reads as a summer topping signal.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: September unlocks on richly-valued AI IPOs are a near-term supply overhang and potential top-tick catalyst.

insights.claim: Hayes states the entire equity market is now purely an AI story where every rally is a second/third derivative of an AI capex buildout he expects to exceed the railroads as a share of GDP — and notes spending is not yet there, implying the bubble is still in its early innings.
insights.speaker: Arthur Hayes
insights.so_what: Implies more upside/blow-off before the top; capex-as-%-of-GDP vs the railroad benchmark is the gauge for how far the bubble can still run.
insights.quote: The equity market is literally just an AI story and any second or third derivative is from this CapEx buildout that is going to be probably bigger than the railroads.
